title: Relate double number lines and ratio tables
description: Use double number lines to create ratio tables.
topic_path: Topic B: Collections of equivalent ratios
Relate double number lines and ratio tables

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows how many model trains Irene can build in $1$ week.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which table represents the rate of Irene building model trains?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The number lines show that the ratio of $\goldE{\text{weeks}}$ to $\maroonD{\text{trains built}}$ is $\goldE1:\maroonD{9}$

We are looking for a table that has ratios that are equivalent to $\goldE1:\maroonD{9}$

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\goldE1:\maroonD{9}$ is equivalent to $\goldE5:\maroonD{45}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\goldE1:\maroonD{9}$ is equivalent to $\goldE{12}:\maroonD{108}$

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\goldE{1}:\maroonD{9}$ is equivalent to $\goldE{26}:\maroonD{234}$.

Here is the table that represents the rate of Irene building model trains:

Weeks | Trains
- | - 
$5$ | $45$
$12$| $108$
$26$ | $234$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Model trains, are 9 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Weeks. The number line labeled Weeks reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Model trains reads from left to right: 0 and 9.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows how many meters a dragonfly can fly in $1$ second.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which table represents the rate at which a dragonfly can fly?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The number lines show that the ratio of $\blueE{\text{seconds}}$ to $\maroonD{\text{meters }}$ is $\blueE{1}:\maroonD{25}$

We are looking for a table that has ratios that are equivalent to $\blueE{1}:\maroonD{25}$

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\blueE{1}:\maroonD{25}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{3}:\maroonD{75}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\blueE{1}:\maroonD{25}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{5}:\maroonD{125}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\blueE{1}:\maroonD{25}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{10}:\maroonD{250}$.

Here is the table that represents the rate at which a dragonfly can fly.

Seconds| Meters
- | - 
$3$ | $75$
$5$| $125$
$10$ | $250$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Distance (meters), are 25 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Time (seconds). The number line labeled Time (seconds) reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Distance (meters) reads from left to right: 0 and 25.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows that $1$ kilogram of chocolate costs $\$4$.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which table represents the cost of chocolate per kilogram?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The number lines show that the ratio of $\goldE{\text{kilograms}}$ to $\maroonD{\text{dollars}}$ is $\goldE1:\maroonD{4}$

We are looking for a table that has ratios that are equivalent to $\goldE1:\maroonD{4}$

[[☃ image 1]]


The ratio of $\goldE1:\maroonD{4}$ is equivalent to $\goldE{7}:\maroonD{28}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\goldE1:\maroonD{4}$ is equivalent to $\goldE{15}:\maroonD{60}$.

[[☃ image 1]]



The ratio of $\goldE{1}:\maroonD{4}$ is equivalent to $\goldE{22}:\maroonD{88}$.

Here is the table that represents the rate of chocolate per kilogram:

Kilograms| Cost (dollars)
- | :-: 
$7$ | $28$
$15$| $60$
$22$ | $88$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Cost (dollars), are 4 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Chocolate (kg). The number line labeled Chocolate (kg) reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Cost (dollars) reads from left to right: 0 and 4.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows that $1$ game of bowling costs $14$ dollars.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which table represents the cost per game of bowling?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The number lines show that the ratio of $\blueE{\text{games}}$ to $\maroonD{\text{dollars}}$ is $\blueE1:\maroonD{14}$.

We are looking for a table that has ratios that are equivalent to $\blueE1:\maroonD{14}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\blueE1:\maroonD{14}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{2}:\maroonD{28}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\blueE1:\maroonD{14}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{4}:\maroonD{56}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\blueE1:\maroonD{14}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{7}:\maroonD{98}$.

Here is the table that represents the cost per game of bowling:

Games | Cost (dollars)
:-: | :-: 
$2$ | $28$
$4$| $56$
$7$ | $98$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Cost (dollars), are 14 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Games. The number line labeled Games reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Cost (dollars) reads from left to right: 0 and 14.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows that a bakery uses $20 \text{ kg}$ of butter in $1$ day.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which table represents the rate at which the bakery uses butter?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The number lines show that the ratio of $\blueE{\text{days}}$ to $\maroonD{\text{kilograms}}$ is $\blueE1:\maroonD{20}$.

We are looking for a table that has ratios that are equivalent to $\blueE1:\maroonD{20}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\blueE{1}:\maroonD{20}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{5}:\maroonD{100}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\blueE{1}:\maroonD{20}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{7}:\maroonD{140}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\blueE{1}:\maroonD{20}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{30}:\maroonD{600}$.

Here is the table that represents the rate at which the bakery uses butter:

Days | Butter ($\text{kg}$)
:-: | :-: 
$5$ | $100$
$7$| $140$
$30$ | $600$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Butter (kg), are 20 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Time (days). The number line labeled Time (days) reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Butter (kg) reads from left to right: 0 and 20.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

Susan plants her tomato plants in rows. The double number line shows that she plants $8$ tomato plants in $1$ row.



**Which table represents the number of tomato plants per row?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The number lines show that the ratio of $\blueE{\text{rows}}$ to $\maroonD{\text{plants}}$ is $\blueE1:\maroonD{8}$.

We are looking for a table that has ratios that are equivalent to $\blueE1:\maroonD{8}$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c524ea52abb9986e0334cff0402eb70424f7712)

The ratio of $\blueE1:\maroonD{8}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{5}:\maroonD{40}$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/80e0bd1a32bb7aba2d559654570e47c62b7cec3e)

The ratio of $\blueE1:\maroonD{8}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{8}:\maroonD{64}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The ratio of $\blueE1:\maroonD{8}$ is equivalent to $\blueE{11}:\maroonD{88}$.

Here is the table that represents the number of tomato plants per row:

Rows | Plants
:-: | :-: 
$5$ | $40$
$8$| $64$
$11$ | $88$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Tomato plants, are 8 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Rows. The number line labeled Rows reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Tomato plants reads from left to right: 0 and 8.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows how many bananas are needed to make $1$ loaf of banana bread.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/369285ce75ad39b988e17d8ffb8a31e2f69c5a52)

**Complete the table to show the same information as the double number line.** 

Number of loaves | Number of bananas 
:-: | :-:
[[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $2\dfrac12$
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $5$ 
$4$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The double number lines show that we need $\blueE{2\dfrac12}$ bananas for $\maroonD1$ loaf of banana bread.


Number of loaves | Number of bananas 
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $2\dfrac12$

The remaining values in the table represent ratios that are equivalent to this ratio.
If we use $2$ times as many bananas, we will make  $2$ times as many loaves.



[[☃ image 1]]



If we make $4$ times as many loaves, we will need $4$ times as many bananas.



[[☃ image 1]]

Here is the completed table:

Number of loaves | Number of bananas 
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $2\dfrac12$
$2$ | $5$ 
$4$ | $10$

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows how many snowballs Jacob and his friends can make in $1$ minute.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table to show the same information as the double number line.** 

Time (minutes)| Number of snowballs
:-: | :-:
[[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $12$
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $48$ 
$5$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The double number lines show that Jacob and his friends can make $\blueE{12}$ snowballs in $\maroonD1$ minute.


Time (minutes)| Number of snowballs
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $12$

The remaining values in the table represent ratios that are equivalent to this ratio.

To make $4$ times as many snowballs, will take $4$ times as many minutes.



[[☃ image 1]]

Taking $5$ times as many minutes, will result in $5$ times as many snowballs.


[[☃ image 1]]

Here is the completed table:

Time (minutes)| Number of snowballs
:-: | :-:
$1$ |$12$
$4$ | $48$ 
$5$ | $60$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Snowballs, are 12 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Time (minutes). The number line labeled Time (minutes) reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Snowballs reads from left to right: 0 and 12.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows how far a hummingbird can fly in $1$ second.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table to show the same information as the double number line.** 

Time (seconds)| Distance (meters)
:-: | :-:
[[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $13$
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $39$ 
$10$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The double number lines show that  a hummingbird can fly $\blueE{13}$ meters in $\maroonD1$ second.


Time (seconds)| Distance (meters)
:-: | :-:
$1$| $13$

The remaining values in the table represent ratios that are equivalent to this ratio.

If the hummingbird flies $3$ times as many meters, it will take $3$ times as many seconds.



[[☃ image 1]]

If the hummingbird flies for  $10$ times as many seconds, it will fly $10$ times as many meters.


[[☃ image 1]]

Here is the completed table:

Time (seconds)| Distance (meters)
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $13$
$3$ | $39$ 
$10$ | $130$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Distance (meters), are 13 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Time (seconds). The number line labeled Time (seconds) reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Distance (meters) reads from left to right: 0 and 13.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows the amount of time a hen needs to lay $1$ egg.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table to show the same information as the double number line.** 

Eggs | Time (hours)
:-: | :-:
$3$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $100$
$12$ | [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
hints:
The double number lines show that  a hen lays $\blueE{1}$ egg every $\maroonD{25}$ hours.


Eggs| Time (hours)
:-: | :-:
$1$| $25$

The remaining values in the table represent ratios that are equivalent to this ratio.
If the hen lays $3$ times as many eggs, it needs $3$ times as many hours.



[[☃ image 1]]

If the hen has $4$ times as many hours, it can lay $4$ times as many eggs.



[[☃ image 1]]

If the hen lays $12$ times as many eggs, it needs $12$ times as many hours.



[[☃ image 1]]

Here is the completed table:

Eggs | Time (hours)
:-: | :-:
$3$ | $75$
$4$ | $100$ 
$12$ | $300$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Time (hours), are 25 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Eggs. The number line labeled Eggs reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Time (hours) reads from left to right: 0 and 25.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows the number of children $1$ adult supervises on a field trip.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table to show the same information as the double number line.** 

Adults | Children
:-: | :-:
$8$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $66$
$15$ | [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
hints:
The double number lines show that $\blueD{1}$ adult supervises $\maroonD{6}$ children.


Adults | Children
:-: | :-:
$1$| $6$

The remaining values in the table represent ratios that are equivalent to this ratio.
If there are $8$ times as many adults, then they can supervise $8$ times as many children.



[[☃ image 1]]

If there are $11$ times as many children, then $11$ times as many adults supervise them.


[[☃ image 1]]

If there are $15$ times as many adults, then they can supervise $15$ times as many children.


[[☃ image 1]]

Here is the completed table:

Adults | Children
:-: | :-:
$8$ | $48$
$11$ | $66$ 
$15$ | $90$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Children, are 6 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Adults. The number line labeled Adults reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Children reads from left to right: 0 and 6.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number line shows the price of  $1$ cupcake.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table to show the same information as the double number line.** 

Cupcakes | Price (dollars)
:-: | :-:
$5$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $18$
$18$ | [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
hints:
The double number lines show that the price of $\blueE{1}$  cupcake is $\maroonD{1.5}$ dollars.


Cupcakes | Price (dollars)
:-: | :-:
$1$| $1.5$

The remaining values in the table represent ratios that are equivalent to this ratio.
For $5$ times as many cupcakes, the price is $5$ times as much.



[[☃ image 1]]

For $12$ times the price, there are $12$ times as many cupcakes.



[[☃ image 1]]

For $18$ times as many cupcakes, the price is $18$ times as much.



[[☃ image 1]]

Here is the completed table:

Cupcakes | Price (dollars)
:-: | :-:
$5$ | $7.50$
$12$ | $18$ 
$18$ | $27$

options.alt: A double number line with 2 tick marks on each number line. The values in the second number line, labeled Price (dollars), are 1.5 times the corresponding values in the first number line, labeled Cupcakes. The number line labeled Cupcakes reads from left to right: 0 and 1. The number line labeled Price (dollars) reads from left to right: 0 and 1.5.
